texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: CytonRL: an Efficient Reinforcement Learning Open-source Toolkit Implemented in C++
texts.text: CytonRL: an Efficient Reinforcement Learning Open-source Toolkit Implemented in C++

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Xiaolin Wang
texts.text: Xiaolin Wang

texts.label: text
texts.orig: National Institute of Information and Communications Technology, Japan xiaolin.wang@nict.go.jp
texts.text: National Institute of Information and Communications Technology, Japan xiaolin.wang@nict.go.jp

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper presents an open-source enforcement learning toolkit named CytonRL 1 . The toolkit implements four recent advanced deep Q-learning algorithms from scratch using C++ and NVIDIA's GPU-accelerated libraries. The code is simple and elegant, owing to an open-source general-purpose neural network library named CytonLib. Benchmark shows that the toolkit achieves competitive performances on the popular Atari game of Breakout.
texts.text: This paper presents an open-source enforcement learning toolkit named CytonRL 1 . The toolkit implements four recent advanced deep Q-learning algorithms from scratch using C++ and NVIDIA's GPU-accelerated libraries. The code is simple and elegant, owing to an open-source general-purpose neural network library named CytonLib. Benchmark shows that the toolkit achieves competitive performances on the popular Atari game of Breakout.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reinforcement learning (RL) is self learning what to do under an environment, in other words, how to map situations to actions, so as to maximize a numerical reward signal (Sutton and Barto, 1998). RL is an meaningful artificial intelligence task, and will be extremely useful if it works. However, traditional real-world RL systems were usually built upon hand-crafted features from raw sensor data, which is a bottleneck of their performance. Therefore, learning to control agents directly from high-dimensional sensory inputs was considered as one of the long-standing challenges of RL.
texts.text: Reinforcement learning (RL) is self learning what to do under an environment, in other words, how to map situations to actions, so as to maximize a numerical reward signal (Sutton and Barto, 1998). RL is an meaningful artificial intelligence task, and will be extremely useful if it works. However, traditional real-world RL systems were usually built upon hand-crafted features from raw sensor data, which is a bottleneck of their performance. Therefore, learning to control agents directly from high-dimensional sensory inputs was considered as one of the long-standing challenges of RL.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently, the deep learning community has developed deep neural networks to automatically extract high-level features from raw sensory data, leading to breakthroughs in computer vision (LeCun et al., 1998; Krizhevsky et al., 2012; Farabet et al., 2013; Sermanet et al., 2013; Mnih, 2013) and speech recognition (Povey et al., 2014; Dahl et al., 2012; Graves et al., 2013). Excitingly, the RL community integreted this technology into their systems, and achieved the long-standing challenge (Mnih et al., 2013, 2015).
texts.text: Recently, the deep learning community has developed deep neural networks to automatically extract high-level features from raw sensory data, leading to breakthroughs in computer vision (LeCun et al., 1998; Krizhevsky et al., 2012; Farabet et al., 2013; Sermanet et al., 2013; Mnih, 2013) and speech recognition (Povey et al., 2014; Dahl et al., 2012; Graves et al., 2013). Excitingly, the RL community integreted this technology into their systems, and achieved the long-standing challenge (Mnih et al., 2013, 2015).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The breakthrough in RL will undoubtedly give birth to impressive progress in the related fields such as natural language processing and robotics. Therefore, we develop the open-source toolkit of CytonRL in the hope to benefit research communities as well as industries.
texts.text: The breakthrough in RL will undoubtedly give birth to impressive progress in the related fields such as natural language processing and robotics. Therefore, we develop the open-source toolkit of CytonRL in the hope to benefit research communities as well as industries.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CytonRL is an open-source toolkit of deep Q-learning. It achieves competitive performances on the test environment of Atari 2600 test-bed (Bellemare et al., 2013) through following the works as,

texts.text: In addition, the parameter settings of CytonRL has been carefully tuned for both efficiency and effectiveness.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2 Method
texts.text: 2 Method

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CytonRL has implemented four recent advanced deep Q-learning algorithms proposed by (Mnih et al., 2013; van Hasselt et al., 2015; Schaul et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015). The following subsections first introduce the background knowledge of reinforcement learning, and then present the details of these four algorithms.
texts.text: CytonRL has implemented four recent advanced deep Q-learning algorithms proposed by (Mnih et al., 2013; van Hasselt et al., 2015; Schaul et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015). The following subsections first introduce the background knowledge of reinforcement learning, and then present the details of these four algorithms.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1 Background
texts.text: 2.1 Background

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Suppose an agent interacts with an environment E in a sequence of actions, observations, and rewards (Mnih et al., 2013). At each time-step,the agent selects an action a t from the set of legal game actions, A = { 1 , . . . , K } . The action is passed to E and modifies its internal state. The agent both receives an reward r t and makes an new observation x t +1 from E .
texts.text: Suppose an agent interacts with an environment E in a sequence of actions, observations, and rewards (Mnih et al., 2013). At each time-step,the agent selects an action a t from the set of legal game actions, A = { 1 , . . . , K } . The action is passed to E and modifies its internal state. The agent both receives an reward r t and makes an new observation x t +1 from E .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most often the observation x t does not fully specify the internal state of E . Therefore, the sequence of actions and observations s t = x 1 , a 1 , x 2 , a 2 , . . . , a t -1 , x t are considered as an representation of E 's state, upon which strategies are learned.
texts.text: Most often the observation x t does not fully specify the internal state of E . Therefore, the sequence of actions and observations s t = x 1 , a 1 , x 2 , a 2 , . . . , a t -1 , x t are considered as an representation of E 's state, upon which strategies are learned.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The goal of the agent is to interact with E by selecting actions in a way that maximizes future rewards. There is an standard assumption that future rewards are discounted by a factor of γ per time-step, as E is generally stochastic. The future discounted return at the time t is defined as
texts.text: The goal of the agent is to interact with E by selecting actions in a way that maximizes future rewards. There is an standard assumption that future rewards are discounted by a factor of γ per time-step, as E is generally stochastic. The future discounted return at the time t is defined as

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: R t = T ∑ t ′ = t γ t ′ -t r t ′ , (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where T is the time-step at which E decides to terminate.
texts.text: where T is the time-step at which E decides to terminate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to deduce the optimal policy, a helper function named optimal action-value function is defined as the maximum expected return achievable by following any strategy, after seeing some sequence s and then taking some action a , formulated as,
texts.text: In order to deduce the optimal policy, a helper function named optimal action-value function is defined as the maximum expected return achievable by following any strategy, after seeing some sequence s and then taking some action a , formulated as,

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Q ∗ ( s, a ) = max π E [ R t | s t = s, a t = a, π ] , (2)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where π is a policy mapping a sequence to actions or distributions over actions.
texts.text: where π is a policy mapping a sequence to actions or distributions over actions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The optimal policy can be derived after knowing Q ∗ ( s, a ) , formulated as,
texts.text: The optimal policy can be derived after knowing Q ∗ ( s, a ) , formulated as,

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: a ∗ ( s ) = argmax a Q ∗ ( s, a ) . (3)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The optimal action-value function obeys the Bellman equation ,
texts.text: The optimal action-value function obeys the Bellman equation ,

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Q ∗ ( s, a ) = E [ r + γ max a ′ Q ∗ ( s ′ , a ′ ) | s, a ] , (4)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where s ′ is any sequence derived by taking a after seeing s .
texts.text: where s ′ is any sequence derived by taking a after seeing s .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2 Deep Q-Network with Experience Replay
texts.text: 2.2 Deep Q-Network with Experience Replay

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deep Q-Network (DQN) uses a neural network to approximate the optimal action-value function Q ∗ ( s, a ) . The network is trained by minimizing a sequence of loss functions at each iteration i , as
texts.text: Deep Q-Network (DQN) uses a neural network to approximate the optimal action-value function Q ∗ ( s, a ) . The network is trained by minimizing a sequence of loss functions at each iteration i , as

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: L i ( θ i ) = E s,a ∼ ρ ( · ) [ ( y i -Q ( s, a ; θ i )) 2 ] (5)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where y i = E s ′ ∼ E [ r + γ max a ′ Q ( s ′ , a ′ ; θ i -1 ) | s, a ] is a boosted target for the iteration i , and ρ ( s, a ) is a probability distribution over s and a referred as behavior distribution. Differentiating the loss functions with respect to the weights leads to,
texts.text: where y i = E s ′ ∼ E [ r + γ max a ′ Q ( s ′ , a ′ ; θ i -1 ) | s, a ] is a boosted target for the iteration i , and ρ ( s, a ) is a probability distribution over s and a referred as behavior distribution. Differentiating the loss functions with respect to the weights leads to,

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ∇ θ i L i ( θ i ) = E s, a ∼ ρ ( . ) s ′ ∼ E [ -( r + γ max a ′ ( s ′ , a ′ ; θ i -1 ) -Q ( s, a ; θ i ) ) ∇ θ i Q ( s, a ; θ i ) ] . (6)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: DQN simplifies the computation through replacing the expection by single samples from the ρ and E , forumlated as,
texts.text: DQN simplifies the computation through replacing the expection by single samples from the ρ and E , forumlated as,

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ∇ θ i L t ( θ i ) = -( r t + γ max a ′ Q ( s t +1 , a ′ ; θ i -1 ) -Q ( s t , a t ; θ i ) ) ∇ θ i Q ( s t , a t ; θ i ) . (7)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (Mnih et al., 2013) proposed utilizing an experience replay technique in DQN, presented by the algorithm 1. The approach stores the agent's experiences at each time-step formulated as e t = ( s t , a t , r t , s t +1 ) , into a replay memory as D = e 1 , . . . , e N . The approach then picks random samples from D for updating the neural network.
texts.text: (Mnih et al., 2013) proposed utilizing an experience replay technique in DQN, presented by the algorithm 1. The approach stores the agent's experiences at each time-step formulated as e t = ( s t , a t , r t , s t +1 ) , into a replay memory as D = e 1 , . . . , e N . The approach then picks random samples from D for updating the neural network.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: DQN with experience replay is dramatically more efficient and stable than the standard online Qlearning (Sutton and Barto, 1998). The reasons are as follows (Mnih et al., 2013).
texts.text: DQN with experience replay is dramatically more efficient and stable than the standard online Qlearning (Sutton and Barto, 1998). The reasons are as follows (Mnih et al., 2013).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Learning directly from consecutive samples is inefficient due to the strong correlations between the samples; randomizing the samples breaks these correlations and therefore reduces the variance of updates.
texts.text: Learning directly from consecutive samples is inefficient due to the strong correlations between the samples; randomizing the samples breaks these correlations and therefore reduces the variance of updates.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · When learning on-policy, the current parameters determine the next data sample that the parameters are trained on; By using experience replay the behavior distribution is averaged over many of its previous states, smoothing out learning and voiding oscillations or divergence in the parameters.
texts.text: When learning on-policy, the current parameters determine the next data sample that the parameters are trained on; By using experience replay the behavior distribution is averaged over many of its previous states, smoothing out learning and voiding oscillations or divergence in the parameters.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Each step of experience is potentially used in many weight updates, which allows for greater data efficiency.
texts.text: Each step of experience is potentially used in many weight updates, which allows for greater data efficiency.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3 Double Deep Q-Network
texts.text: 2.3 Double Deep Q-Network

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (van Hasselt et al., 2015) proposed double DQN to reduce the over-estimations caused by the max operation in the equation 7.
texts.text: (van Hasselt et al., 2015) proposed double DQN to reduce the over-estimations caused by the max operation in the equation 7.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The standard DQN used a training target as,
texts.text: The standard DQN used a training target as,

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Y DQN t = r t + γ max a Q ( s t +1 , a ; θ -t ) , (8)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where θ -t is the parameters of a target network which is copied periodically from the online network. Because DQN is a kind of boosting algorithm, the estimated target Q ( s t +1 , a ; θ -t ) is unavoidably inaccurate as an oracle function during the training procedure. This inaccuracy is high likely to be converted into over-estimations by the max operation.
texts.text: where θ -t is the parameters of a target network which is copied periodically from the online network. Because DQN is a kind of boosting algorithm, the estimated target Q ( s t +1 , a ; θ -t ) is unavoidably inaccurate as an oracle function during the training procedure. This inaccuracy is high likely to be converted into over-estimations by the max operation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Double DQN decomposes the max operation in the equation 8 into action selection and action evaluation, formulated as,
texts.text: Double DQN decomposes the max operation in the equation 8 into action selection and action evaluation, formulated as,

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Y DoubleDQN t = r t + γQ ( s t +1 , argmaxQ ( s t +1 , a ; θ t ) , θ -t ) , (9)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where the online network with the parameters θ t is used to evaluate the greedy policy, and the target network with the parameters θ -t is used to estimate its values.
texts.text: where the online network with the parameters θ t is used to evaluate the greedy policy, and the target network with the parameters θ -t is used to estimate its values.

texts.label: code
texts.orig: Algorithm 1 Deep Q-Network with Experience Replay 1: Initialize the replay memory D to capacity N 2: Initialize the action-value function Q with random weights 3: for episode = 1 , M do 4: Initialize sequence s 1 = { x 1 } and preprocessed φ 1 = φ ( s 1 ) 5: for t = 1 , T do 6: With probability ǫ select a random action a t 7: otherwise select a t = argmax a Q ∗ ( φ ( s t ) , a ; θ ) 8: Execute action a t in emulator and observe reward r t and image x t +1 9: Set s t +1 = s t , a t , x t +1 and preprocess φ t +1 = φ ( s t +1 ) 10: Store transition ( φ t , a t , r t , φ t +1 ) in D 11: Sample random minibatch of transitions ( φ j , a j , r j , φ j +1 ) from D 12: Set y j = { r j for terminal φ j +1 r j + γ max a ′ Q ( φ j +1 , a ′ ; θ ) for non-terminal φ j +1 13: Perform a gradient descent step on ( y j -Q ( φ j a j ; θ )) 2 using the equation 7 14: end for 15: end for
texts.text: Algorithm 1 Deep Q-Network with Experience Replay 1: Initialize the replay memory D to capacity N 2: Initialize the action-value function Q with random weights 3: for episode = 1 , M do 4: Initialize sequence s 1 = { x 1 } and preprocessed φ 1 = φ ( s 1 ) 5: for t = 1 , T do 6: With probability ǫ select a random action a t 7: otherwise select a t = argmax a Q ∗ ( φ ( s t ) , a ; θ ) 8: Execute action a t in emulator and observe reward r t and image x t +1 9: Set s t +1 = s t , a t , x t +1 and preprocess φ t +1 = φ ( s t +1 ) 10: Store transition ( φ t , a t , r t , φ t +1 ) in D 11: Sample random minibatch of transitions ( φ j , a j , r j , φ j +1 ) from D 12: Set y j = { r j for terminal φ j +1 r j + γ max a ′ Q ( φ j +1 , a ′ ; θ ) for non-terminal φ j +1 13: Perform a gradient descent step on ( y j -Q ( φ j a j ; θ )) 2 using the equation 7 14: end for 15: end for

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4 Prioritized Experience Replay
texts.text: 2.4 Prioritized Experience Replay

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (Schaul et al., 2015) proposed prioritized experience replay to improve the learning efficiency of DQN, presented by the algorithm 2. The intuition of the method is to replay important transitions more frequently.
texts.text: (Schaul et al., 2015) proposed prioritized experience replay to improve the learning efficiency of DQN, presented by the algorithm 2. The intuition of the method is to replay important transitions more frequently.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The probability of sampling a transition i is defined as,
texts.text: The probability of sampling a transition i is defined as,

texts.orig: P ( i ) = p α i k p α k (10)

texts.label: text

texts.text: ∑ where p i > 0 is the priority of the transition i . The exponent α determines how much prioritization is used, with α = 0 corresponding to the uniform case.

texts.text: The priority p i in the proportional prioritization method, which is implemented in CytonRL, is defined as,

texts.text: where δ i is the prediction error, and ǫ is a small positive constant that prevents the edge-case of transitions not being revisited once their error is zero.

texts.text: Prioritized experience replay changes the sampling distribution, which brings bias to the estimation. Importance-sampling is used to compensate this bias, formulated as

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.5 Dueling DQN
texts.text: 2.5 Dueling DQN

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (Wang et al., 2015) proposed an dueling neural network architecture for DQN, which decomposed the Q function into two separate estimators; one for the state-value function and one for the state-dependent action-advantage function.
texts.text: (Wang et al., 2015) proposed an dueling neural network architecture for DQN, which decomposed the Q function into two separate estimators; one for the state-value function and one for the state-dependent action-advantage function.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Q function of dueling DQN is formulated as,
texts.text: The Q function of dueling DQN is formulated as,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where V ( s ; θ, β ) is an state-value function, and A ( s, a ; θ, α ) is an state-dependent action-advantage function. Note that the formula makes that the action-advantage function has no overall impact on the state-value function. In this way, the two functions can be uniquely derived from any Q function.
texts.text: where V ( s ; θ, β ) is an state-value function, and A ( s, a ; θ, α ) is an state-dependent action-advantage function. Note that the formula makes that the action-advantage function has no overall impact on the state-value function. In this way, the two functions can be uniquely derived from any Q function.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Algorithm 2 Double DQN with Proportional Prioritized Experience Replay
texts.text: Algorithm 2 Double DQN with Proportional Prioritized Experience Replay

texts.label: code
texts.orig: Require: minibatch k , step-size η , replay period K and size N , exponents α and β , budget T . 1: Initialize replay memory H to capacity N , ∆ = 0 , p 1 = 1 . 2: Observe s 1 3: for t = 1 , T do 4: Choose action a t ∼ π θ ( s t ) 5: Observe r t and s t +1 6: Store transition ( s t , a t , r t , s t +1 ) in H with maximal priority p i = max i<t p i 7: if t = 0 mod K then 8: for j = 1 , k do 9: Sample transition j ∼ P ( j ) = p α j / ∑ i p α i 10: Compute importance-sampling weight w j = ( N · P ( j )) -β /max i w i 11: Compute TD-error δ j = r j + γQ target ( s j +1 , argmax a Q ( s j , a )) -Q ( s j , a j ) 12: Update transition priority p j ←| δ j | 13: Accumulate weight-change ∆ ← ∆+ w j · δ j · ∇ θ Q ( s j , a j ) 14: end for 15: Update weights θ ← θ + η · ∆ 16: ∆ ← 0 . 17: From the time to time copy wiehgts into target network θ target ← θ 18: end if 19: end for
texts.text: Require: minibatch k , step-size η , replay period K and size N , exponents α and β , budget T . 1: Initialize replay memory H to capacity N , ∆ = 0 , p 1 = 1 . 2: Observe s 1 3: for t = 1 , T do 4: Choose action a t ∼ π θ ( s t ) 5: Observe r t and s t +1 6: Store transition ( s t , a t , r t , s t +1 ) in H with maximal priority p i = max i<t p i 7: if t = 0 mod K then 8: for j = 1 , k do 9: Sample transition j ∼ P ( j ) = p α j / ∑ i p α i 10: Compute importance-sampling weight w j = ( N · P ( j )) -β /max i w i 11: Compute TD-error δ j = r j + γQ target ( s j +1 , argmax a Q ( s j , a )) -Q ( s j , a j ) 12: Update transition priority p j ←| δ j | 13: Accumulate weight-change ∆ ← ∆+ w j · δ j · ∇ θ Q ( s j , a j ) 14: end for 15: Update weights θ ← θ + η · ∆ 16: ∆ ← 0 . 17: From the time to time copy wiehgts into target network θ target ← θ 18: end if 19: end for

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 Implementation
texts.text: 3 Implementation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CytonRL is implemented using the C++ language with a dependency on OpenCV 2 to down-sample the input images, and a dependency on NVIDIA's GPU-accelerated libraries cuda , cublas and cudnn to use GPUs. CytonLib - a general purpose C++ neural network library - is shipped together with the toolkit, which greatly reduced the workload of writing C++ codes for GPUs.
texts.text: CytonRL is implemented using the C++ language with a dependency on OpenCV 2 to down-sample the input images, and a dependency on NVIDIA's GPU-accelerated libraries cuda , cublas and cudnn to use GPUs. CytonLib - a general purpose C++ neural network library - is shipped together with the toolkit, which greatly reduced the workload of writing C++ codes for GPUs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The neural network architecture used by CytonRL is illustrated by the figure 1, which have been established for the Atari games (Mnih et al., 2013, 2015). The input of the neural network is processed Atari frames. The raw Atari frames are 210 × 100 pixel images with a 128 color palette. The images are converted to grey-scale, and linearly interpreted into 84 × 84 through the OpenCV library. The consecutive 4 images are concatenated to form an 84 × 84 × 4 tensor, which is taken as the input to the neural network. The structures of each layer in the neural network are as follows.
texts.text: The neural network architecture used by CytonRL is illustrated by the figure 1, which have been established for the Atari games (Mnih et al., 2013, 2015). The input of the neural network is processed Atari frames. The raw Atari frames are 210 × 100 pixel images with a 128 color palette. The images are converted to grey-scale, and linearly interpreted into 84 × 84 through the OpenCV library. The consecutive 4 images are concatenated to form an 84 × 84 × 4 tensor, which is taken as the input to the neural network. The structures of each layer in the neural network are as follows.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The first layer uses convolution connections with a 8 × 8 × 4 ↦→ 32 fi lter with a stride of (4, 4).
texts.text: The first layer uses convolution connections with a 8 × 8 × 4 ↦→ 32 fi lter with a stride of (4, 4).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The second layer uses convolution connections with a 4 × 4 × 32 ↦→ 64 fi lter with a stride of (2, 2).
texts.text: The second layer uses convolution connections with a 4 × 4 × 32 ↦→ 64 fi lter with a stride of (2, 2).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The third layer uses convolution connections with a 3 × 3 × 64 ↦→ 64 fi lter with a stride of (1,1).
texts.text: The third layer uses convolution connections with a 3 × 3 × 64 ↦→ 64 fi lter with a stride of (1,1).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The fourth layer uses full connections with 512 units.
texts.text: The fourth layer uses full connections with 512 units.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The fifth layer uses full connections with the same number of units as the target signals.
texts.text: The fifth layer uses full connections with the same number of units as the target signals.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The activation functions of all layers are rectified linear function (Nair and Hinton, 2010).
texts.text: The activation functions of all layers are rectified linear function (Nair and Hinton, 2010).

texts.orig: 4.1 Settings
texts.text: 4.1 Settings

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The hyperparameter settings of CytonRL used in the benchmarks are presented by the table 1, which are coded as the default settings. The settings are initially based on the (Mnih et al., 2013; van Hasselt et al., 2015; Schaul et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015), and modified to improve the stability and efficiency according to our experiments.
texts.text: The hyperparameter settings of CytonRL used in the benchmarks are presented by the table 1, which are coded as the default settings. The settings are initially based on the (Mnih et al., 2013; van Hasselt et al., 2015; Schaul et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015), and modified to improve the stability and efficiency according to our experiments.

texts.label: code
texts.orig: class NetworkRL: public Network { ConvolutionLayer conv1; // declare components ActivationLayer act1; ConvolutionLayer conv2; ActivationLayer act2; ConvolutionLayer conv3; ActivationLayer act3; LinearLayer lin1; ActivationLayer act4; DueLinearLayer dueLin2; LinearLayer lin2; void init(Variable* x, int nTarget) // x: input of the neural network which is image data // nTarget: dimension of output which equals to the number of // control signals { tx=conv1.init(tx, 32, 8, 8, 4, 4, 0, 0); layers.push_back(&conv1); tx=act1.init(tx, CUDNN_ACTIVATION_RELU); layers.push_back(&act1); tx=conv2.init(tx, 64, 4, 4, 2, 2, 0, 0); layers.push_back(&conv2); tx=act2.init(tx, CUDNN_ACTIVATION_RELU); layers.push_back(&act2); tx=conv3.init(tx, 64, 3, 3, 1, 1, 0, 0); layers.push_back(&conv3); tx=act3.init(tx, CUDNN_ACTIVATION_RELU); layers.push_back(&act3); tx=lin1.init(tx, 512, true ); layers.push_back(&lin1); tx=act4.act4(tx, CUDNN_ACTIVATION_RELU); layers.push_back(&act4); if(params.dueling) { tx=dueLin2.init(tx, nTarget, true); layers.push_back(&dueLin2); } else { tx=lin2.init(tx, nTarget, true); layers.push_back(&lin2); } return tx; //pointer to result } };
texts.text: class NetworkRL: public Network { ConvolutionLayer conv1; // declare components ActivationLayer act1; ConvolutionLayer conv2; ActivationLayer act2; ConvolutionLayer conv3; ActivationLayer act3; LinearLayer lin1; ActivationLayer act4; DueLinearLayer dueLin2; LinearLayer lin2; void init(Variable* x, int nTarget) // x: input of the neural network which is image data // nTarget: dimension of output which equals to the number of // control signals { tx=conv1.init(tx, 32, 8, 8, 4, 4, 0, 0); layers.push_back(&conv1); tx=act1.init(tx, CUDNN_ACTIVATION_RELU); layers.push_back(&act1); tx=conv2.init(tx, 64, 4, 4, 2, 2, 0, 0); layers.push_back(&conv2); tx=act2.init(tx, CUDNN_ACTIVATION_RELU); layers.push_back(&act2); tx=conv3.init(tx, 64, 3, 3, 1, 1, 0, 0); layers.push_back(&conv3); tx=act3.init(tx, CUDNN_ACTIVATION_RELU); layers.push_back(&act3); tx=lin1.init(tx, 512, true ); layers.push_back(&lin1); tx=act4.act4(tx, CUDNN_ACTIVATION_RELU); layers.push_back(&act4); if(params.dueling) { tx=dueLin2.init(tx, nTarget, true); layers.push_back(&dueLin2); } else { tx=lin2.init(tx, nTarget, true); layers.push_back(&lin2); } return tx; //pointer to result } };

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 2: Source Code of Neural Network
texts.text: Figure 2: Source Code of Neural Network

texts.label: code
texts.orig: class Layer { virtual void forward(){}; virtual void backward(){}; virtual void calculateGradient(){}; }; class Network: public Layer { vector<Layer*> layers; void forward() { for(int k=0; k<layers.size(); k++) layers.at(k)->forward(); } void backward() { for(int k=layers.size()-1; k>=0; k--) layers.at(k)->backward(); } void calculateGradient() { for(int k=layers.size()-1; k>=0; k--) layers.at(k)->calculateGradient(); } };
texts.text: class Layer { virtual void forward(){}; virtual void backward(){}; virtual void calculateGradient(){}; }; class Network: public Layer { vector<Layer*> layers; void forward() { for(int k=0; k<layers.size(); k++) layers.at(k)->forward(); } void backward() { for(int k=layers.size()-1; k>=0; k--) layers.at(k)->backward(); } void calculateGradient() { for(int k=layers.size()-1; k>=0; k--) layers.at(k)->calculateGradient(); } };

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 3: Source Code of Network Class
texts.text: Figure 3: Source Code of Network Class

texts.label: code
texts.orig: void LinearLayer::forward() { cublasXgemm(cublasH, CUBLAS_OP_T, CUBLAS_OP_N, dimOutput, num, dimInput, &one, w.data, w.ni, x.data, dimInput, &zero, y.data, dimOutput) } void LinearLayer::backward() { cublasXgemm(cublasH, CUBLAS_OP_N, CUBLAS_OP_N, dimInput, num, dimOutput, &one, w.data, w.ni, y.grad.data, dimOutput, &beta, x.grad.data, dimInput)); } void LinearLayer::calculateGradient() { cublasXgemm(cublasH, CUBLAS_OP_N, CUBLAS_OP_T, dimInput, dimOutput, num, &one, x.data, dimInput, y.grad.data, dimOutput, &one, w.grad.data, w.grad.ni)); } void ActivationLayer::forward() { cudnnActivationForward(global.cudnnHandle, activeDesc, &global.one, x->desc, x->data, &global.zero, y.desc, y.data) ); }
texts.text: void LinearLayer::forward() { cublasXgemm(cublasH, CUBLAS_OP_T, CUBLAS_OP_N, dimOutput, num, dimInput, &one, w.data, w.ni, x.data, dimInput, &zero, y.data, dimOutput) } void LinearLayer::backward() { cublasXgemm(cublasH, CUBLAS_OP_N, CUBLAS_OP_N, dimInput, num, dimOutput, &one, w.data, w.ni, y.grad.data, dimOutput, &beta, x.grad.data, dimInput)); } void LinearLayer::calculateGradient() { cublasXgemm(cublasH, CUBLAS_OP_N, CUBLAS_OP_T, dimInput, dimOutput, num, &one, x.data, dimInput, y.grad.data, dimOutput, &one, w.grad.data, w.grad.ni)); } void ActivationLayer::forward() { cudnnActivationForward(global.cudnnHandle, activeDesc, &global.one, x->desc, x->data, &global.zero, y.desc, y.data) ); }

texts.orig: For echo model setting, CytonRL was trained 100,000,000 steps, and tested very 5,000,000 steps. In each test, 100 games were played, and the rewards of all games were averaged. The curves of training steps versus average test reward per game are presented in the figure.
texts.text: For echo model setting, CytonRL was trained 100,000,000 steps, and tested very 5,000,000 steps. In each test, 100 games were played, and the rewards of all games were averaged. The curves of training steps versus average test reward per game are presented in the figure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results reveal the strength of each model settings as dueling double DQN with prior. ≻ double DQN with prior. ≻ dueling double DQN ≻ double DQN . Stronger model settings tend to learn the game faster, and achieve better final performance. The results confirm that DQN , double DQN , prioritized relay , and dueling DQN are all effective RL algorithms.
texts.text: The results reveal the strength of each model settings as dueling double DQN with prior. ≻ double DQN with prior. ≻ dueling double DQN ≻ double DQN . Stronger model settings tend to learn the game faster, and achieve better final performance. The results confirm that DQN , double DQN , prioritized relay , and dueling DQN are all effective RL algorithms.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 Conclusion
texts.text: 5 Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper introduces CytonRL - an open-source reinforcement learning toolkit built from scratch using C++ and NVIDA's GPU-accelerated libraries. CytonRL is coded and tuned to achieve competitive performances in a fast manner. In other words, the toolkit is both effective and efficient. The source code of CytonRL is simple because of CytonLib - an open-source general purpose neural network library which is contained in the toolkit. Therefore, CytonRL is an attractive alternative choice for the research community. We open-source this toolkit in the hope to benefit the community and promote the field. We look forward to hearing feedback.
texts.text: This paper introduces CytonRL - an open-source reinforcement learning toolkit built from scratch using C++ and NVIDA's GPU-accelerated libraries. CytonRL is coded and tuned to achieve competitive performances in a fast manner. In other words, the toolkit is both effective and efficient. The source code of CytonRL is simple because of CytonLib - an open-source general purpose neural network library which is contained in the toolkit. Therefore, CytonRL is an attractive alternative choice for the research community. We open-source this toolkit in the hope to benefit the community and promote the field. We look forward to hearing feedback.
